summary: McKenzie explains his decision to switch payment processing from Payloadz to e-junkie for his software business. The three driving factors are automatic post-purchase redirection for analytics and onboarding, simpler flat-rate pricing versus Payloadz's tiered model, and full control over customer-facing communications. He notes that neither processor's branding conveys the professional image he wants. He also calculates that building his own solution isn't worth the time given e-junkie's $5/month cost.
key_points: Post-purchase redirect capability is critical for tracking conversions and improving customer onboarding
Flat-rate pricing ($5/month) is preferable to percentage-based tiers that penalize growth
Controlling the entire customer experience—emails, branding, tone—matters for perceived professionalism
Build-vs-buy decisions should factor in the real cost of developer time against ongoing fees
money_quotes: I don't know where my monthly sales are going to stabilize and don't want them stabilizing at a point where I routinely pay 12-15% of sales to somebody doing, well, essentially not a heck of a lot for me.
Neither Payloadz nor e-junkie screams 'responsible businessman of the sort I am glad to pay to get educational materials from', which is sort of what I'm going for.
I figure that will take me 2 hours, and $5 a month is not really worth 2 hours of my time…
Its absolutely crucial to me that I be able to redirect customers back to my site after the transaction is completed.
e-junkie let me write my own email template, soup to nuts.
key_insight: When choosing payment infrastructure, optimize for customer experience control and predictable pricing over raw features, because your processor's limitations become your brand's limitations.
